content.body: At an early age, Don Lewis heard an orchestra on the radio and wanted to be a conductor—to make music with the orchestra’s huge range of sounds and their nearly limitless combinations. Though he had little access to an orchestra, let alone the means to train as a conductor, Lewis’ church participation brought him to the organ, an instrument of immense flexibility. While he learned to play the organ, Lewis nurtured an interest in electronics and engineering. By his senior year of high school, he had begun to develop plans for his own electronic organ.
content.title: Early Inspirations

content.body: After training as a nuclear weapons specialist in the Air Force and a stint at Honeywell, Lewis began working at a Hammond Organ dealer in Denver in 1968. Lewis played Hammond’s newest organ, the X-77, in local night clubs. The X-77 lacked a built-in rhythm function, limiting Lewis’ ability to fully realize the music he wanted to create. As a solution, he modified an Ace Tone Rhythm Ace FR-2L rhythm machine to work with the X-77’s expression pedal. Before long, Lewis was exploring ways to integrate new instruments into his performance setup.
content.title: Engineering a Solution

content.body: In 1974, Lewis began designing LEO, the Live Electronic Orchestra. In 1977, with the assistance of engineer Richard Bates, Lewis developed the means to control a huge range of instruments—four Oberheim Synthesizer Expander Modules, two ARP 2600s, a Roland Jupiter 4, an Ace Tone FR-7L Rhythm Unit, a Roland Space Echo, and more—into a single instrument that could be played at the same time by one musician. Though he hadn’t become the conductor of an orchestra, he had built his own electronic orchestra, one that allowed him to perform with almost any sound that he could dream of.
content.title: The Design Begins

content.body:
Working in the early 1970s, Don Lewis’ idea for combining multiple synthesizers into a single playable setup faced numerous technical and logistical challenges. LEO represents a huge achievement, comprising many individual solutions, driven by a relentless personal vision. 

As the commercial market for synthesizers developed over the latter 1960s and early 1970s, manufacturers often made sure that many of their own products could be used together—but not necessarily with the products from other manufacturers. Many synthesizers used “control voltage,” or CV, as a way to send information from one unit to another. A common implementation of CV allowed a synthesizer to communicate what note it was playing by producing a specific voltage. Manufacturers tended to use conceptually similar implementations, but the specifics were often different enough to prevent their products from working with each other. One of the first hurdles that Lewis and engineer Richard Bates had to pass while designing LEO was determining how to control the pitch and mix of synthesizers made by ARP, Oberheim, and Roland from the familiar interface of an organ console. LEO features three manuals: the top is a Pascetta four-note polyphonic keyboard, the middle is a Hammond Concorde manual, and the bottom a Hammond Concorde manual retrofitted to control an ARP Pro Soloist with its top three octaves.
content.title: Working Together

content.body:
Working in the early 1970s, Don Lewis’ idea for combining multiple synthesizers into a single playable setup faced numerous technical and logistical challenges. LEO represents a huge achievement, comprising many individual solutions, driven by a relentless personal vision. 

As the commercial market for synthesizers developed over the latter 1960s and early 1970s, manufacturers often made sure that many of their own products could be used together—but not necessarily with the products from other manufacturers. Many synthesizers used “control voltage,” or CV, as a way to send information from one unit to another. A common implementation of CV allowed a synthesizer to communicate what note it was playing by producing a specific voltage. Manufacturers tended to use conceptually similar implementations, but the specifics were often different enough to prevent their products from working with each other. One of the first hurdles that Lewis and engineer Richard Bates had to pass while designing LEO was determining how to control the pitch and mix of synthesizers made by ARP, Oberheim, and Roland from the familiar interface of an organ console. LEO features three manuals: the top is a Pascetta four-note polyphonic keyboard, the middle is a Hammond Concorde manual, and the bottom a Hammond Concorde manual retrofitted to control an ARP Pro Soloist with its top three octaves.
content.title: Getting the Right Mix

content.body:
Ikutaro Kakehashi, founder of Roland Corporation, met Don Lewis in 1969 while he was running his first company, Ace Electronic Industries. Lewis had modified one of Kakehashi’s products—an Ace Tone Rhythm Ace FR-2L rhythm machine—to work with a Hammond X-77 organ. Kakehashi was thoroughly impressed, and a lifelong friendship resulted. 

Lewis was frequently consulted for the development of both new rhythm units and synthesizers for Ace Electronics and later for Roland, which was founded in 1972. (Roland’s first product, the TR-77 rhythm unit, was named as a nod to Lewis’ Hammond X-77.) 

Kakehashi saw Lewis performing with LEO in San Francisco in 1979, and was blown away—not only by Lewis’ musicianship and engineering prowess but also by the obvious challenges that Lewis had needed to overcome in order to realize his musical dream. Kakehashi understood that a system for easily connecting the electronic products from different manufacturers was critical for making musicians’ lives easier and for ensuring the music products industry’s future growth.
content.title: Inspired Connections

content.body:
In 1981, Kakehashi began reaching out to industry leaders, notably Sequential Circuits president Dave Smith. Soon, members of Roland, Yamaha, Korg, Kawai, and Sequential were all contributing to what would be called the Musical Instrument Digital Interface, or simply MIDI. 

Introduced publicly in January 1983 at the NAMM Show, MIDI drove a massive expansion of the production and sales of electronic musical instruments. In 1982, total synthesizer sales in the US numbered approximately 43,000 units, accounting for $50 million. By 1989, sales had reached 322,000 units at a value of $322 million. 

More than synthesizers, though, MIDI allowed musicians to connect any manner of equipment together, including then-new personal computers. In-home production and recording of music at a professional level was suddenly possible, and studios could become incredibly flexible and adaptable.
content.title: The MIDI Movement

content.label: Don Lewis and the Live Electronic Orchestra

body: LEO—the Live Electronic Orchestra—was designed and built by Don Lewis between 1974 and 1977. At the time, electronic instruments made by different manufacturers didn't work reliably, or even at all, with each other. Driven by his lifelong dream of conducting an orchestra, Don leveraged his engineering abilities to develop the solutions needed to create his own orchestra of synthesizers and other electronic instruments. His innovations helped to inspire the development of MIDI, or Musical Instrument Digital Interface. This industry standard both dramatically simplified the process of combining multiple electronic instruments and hugely expanded the market for them.
title: Live Electronic Orchestra
